summary: Storable is a fully distributed company — its own careers page says US staff are fully remote, living in 41 states. So unlike a store or factory workforce, members can't be placed by commute patterns around worksites. Instead, this page starts from where employees are observed to live (professional-network location data), and fills in the fine grain with where Information-sector workers live within each state.

The result is a residence model: eleven states hold about two-thirds of members (Texas and North Carolina lead — the legacy merger sites persist as residence patterns), and the rest spread across the ~37 states where the company is registered to hire.

moves.headline: State shares from observed employee residence
moves.detail: LinkedIn company-page location facets (762 associated members, 591 US) give observed state counts for the 11 states holding ≥2% of members (400/591 = 67.7%). The remaining 32.3% is spread across the registered-hiring states in proportion to each state's Information-sector residents.

citations.label: Storable careers page — 'fully remote in the U.S.'
Registered-hiring states: Built In Austin job posting

moves.headline: Within states: members live where Information-sector workers live
moves.detail: Tract weights = 80% share of the state's NAICS-51-employed residents + 20% share of all working-age residents. The 20% support blend reflects customer-support/ops staff, who are under-represented on LinkedIn and less tech-metro concentrated; the blend weight is bounded by the coverage accounting in the evidence record (~15–25%).

citations.label: US_2025.v0 synthetic population — workplace NAICS by residence tract

moves.headline: No commute anchor — deliberately
moves.detail: The legacy offices (Austin, Wake Forest, Westwood KS, St. George) appear as residence concentrations, not commute destinations. Modeled office-anchored share: zero.

limitations: LinkedIn over-represents professional staff and under-represents hourly support staff; the support blend partially corrects this.
State counts are a 2026 snapshot including some former employees; shares, not absolute counts, carry the signal.
Tail states (each <2% of members) are modeled, not observed.
